channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Air Canada Pilot Accused of Flying for 17 Years Without Proper License
item.description: The pilot, who retired last year before the investigation, held some valid flight credentials, officials said, but not the one required to be a captain.
item.dc:creator: Mack Liederman
item.media:credit: Sammy Kogan/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: An Air Canada pilot was the captain for 900 flights between 2009 and 2025 despite not having the proper license, Canadian officials said.

item.title: G.M. Plans to Develop Energy Storage Batteries as E.V. Sales Flag
item.description: The carmaker will follow Tesla in making components for large batteries used by electric utilities, data centers and other businesses to handle fluctuations in power supply and demand.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.media:description: The main active ingredient in the storage batteries that General Motors plans to produce is sodium, which is cheaper and easier to process than lithium.

item.title: Paramount CEO David Ellison Pledges Editorial Independence for ‘60 Minutes’
item.description: David Ellison told Ms. Stahl, one of three remaining correspondents for the news program, that he would respect the show’s editorial decisions after a tumultuous stretch.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin and Michael M. Grynbaum

item.title: Social Security Benefits Could Be Cut in 6 Years Unless Congress Acts
item.description: The trust fund for the program, which supports roughly 68 million Americans, is on schedule to be depleted in the next six years. Benefits could be cut on average by 22 percent.
item.dc:creator: Tara Siegel Bernard and Margot Sanger-Katz
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: To avoid a benefit reduction, lawmakers would need to raise taxes or reduce checks.

item.title: Billionaires’ Billions Are Increasing Faster Than Ever
item.description: Elon Musk’s potential new status as a trillionaire demonstrates in real time why there has been such a rapid rise in the concentration of wealth at the top.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Cohen

item.title: Dutch Authorities Block $115 Million Deal by a U.S. Tech Company
item.description: The Netherlands blocked a U.S. company from buying a Dutch firm that handles its national ID system, saying it would create a “threat to the public interest.”
item.dc:creator: Adam Satariano and Ana Swanson
item.media:credit: Laurens Van Putten/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Willemijn Aerdts, the Dutch minister for the digital economy and sovereignty, spoke to the news media last month after blocking the acquisition of Solvinity, a Dutch tech company, by the U.S. firm Kyndryl.

item.title: Why Apple’s A.I. Upgrade for Siri Won’t Be Available in Europe
item.description: A regulatory dispute has indefinitely delayed the release of Siri AI.
item.dc:creator: Adam Satariano
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: The keynote at Apple headquarters in Cupertino, Calif., on Monday.

item.title: U.S. Exports Rose in April, as War with Iran Buoyed Oil Demand
item.description: Commerce Department data showed exports rose in April, slightly outpacing the growth in imports.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Paul Ratje for The New York Times
item.media:description: In April, booming U.S. oil exports because of the closure of the Strait of Hormuz more than accounted for the drop-off in the trade deficit.

item.title: OpenAI Tests Investor Appetite for Yet Another Giant I.P.O.
item.description: Some analysts are wondering whether the market can absorb the artificial intelligence giant’s planned stock offering — along with those of SpaceX and Anthropic.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly and Brian O’Keefe
item.media:credit: Mike Kai Chen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sam Altman is officially taking OpenAI public — but what will demand look like for its I.P.O.?

item.title: Stocks Drop on Tech Fears and War Worries
item.description: Oil prices fell after Israel and Iran agreed to halt strikes against each other, easing fears of a broader regional conflict that had briefly driven crude higher.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Dubai’s Economic Endurance Is Being Tested in War With Iran
item.description: As conflict ripples through the Persian Gulf, Dubai’s dependence on trade and travel has become an economic vulnerability.
item.dc:creator: Alex Travelli and Anjana Sankar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Natalie Naccache for The New York Times
item.media:description: Dubai, unlike its oil-dependent neighbors, built an economy on trade, tourism, finance and logistics.

item.title: As Screwworm Cases Mount, U.S. Officials Ramp Up Response
item.description: The parasitic, flesh-eating fly has now been confirmed in cows, goats and dogs.
item.dc:creator: K. R. Callaway and Emily Anthes
item.media:description: The New World screwworm fly. It lays eggs in the open wounds or orifices of warm-blooded animals; the resulting larvae burrow into and expand the wound, consuming the animal’s tissue.

item.title: Hoyle Schweitzer, Who Brought Windsurfing to the Masses, Dies at 93
item.description: With his friend Jim Drake, he built the Windsurfer, a sailboard that was cheaper and more portable than most sailboats. It became a global phenomenon.
item.dc:creator: Daniel E. Slotnik
item.media:description: Hoyle Schweitzer in the early 1970s. The board that he and Jim Drake created had an asymmetrical sail and a hand-held boom, making it possible to ride on waters too calm or choppy for surfing.

item.title: How Banks Are Using the SpaceX IPO to Woo the Super Rich
item.description: Wall Street is giving its richest clients velvet rope access to this week’s public listing of SpaceX, reflecting the growing importance of the wealth management business.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Callaghan O'Hare for The New York Times
item.media:description: SpaceX’s I.P.O. will be an opportunity for banks to nurture their relationships with their richest clients and develop new ones, too.

item.title: The Iran War is Forcing Energy-Importing Countries to Turn Inward
item.description: The Iran war is pushing countries to prioritize domestic energy in order to protect themselves from volatile oil and natural gas markets.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Jes Aznar for The New York Times
item.media:description: The president of Solaric, a rooftop-solar installer based near Manila, said people were now seeing the technology as “a way to survive this new age.”

item.title: Trump Eyes a Piece of A.I. Giants
item.description: The president has publicly weighed the government taking stakes in artificial intelligence giants, as his position on regulating the industry shifts.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.media:description: President Trump has signaled he wants to see greater regulation of artificial intelligence.

item.title: Europe Watches Its Economic Recovery Fade Into the Distance
item.description: As the war in Iran persists, signs point to a prolonged period of higher prices and slower growth rather than a quick shock.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development
item.media:credit: Violette Franchi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A supermarket in Paris. For Europe, the economic drag is now forecast to last into next year.

item.title: Have a Thorny Medical Question? Your Doctor May Be Using A.I. for That.
item.description: OpenEvidence, a fast-growing start-up, is using artificial intelligence to help doctors find answers to clinical questions for diagnosis and treatment.
item.dc:creator: Steve Lohr
item.media:credit: Sasha Maslov for The New York Times

item.title: Oil Rises as Hostilities Escalate Between Iran and Israel
item.description: Oil prices jumped and stocks plunged after Iran and Israel exchanged strikes, raising doubts about the future of fragile cease-fire in the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Kars4Kids Can Continue Broadcasting Ads in California, Appeals Court Says
item.description: A lower court last month found that the ads from the charity, known for its ear-worm jingle, had violated false-advertising laws.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra E. Petri
item.media:description: Kars4Kids primarily funds a New Jersey-based Jewish organization, Oorah.

item.title: ‘Scary Movie’ Returns Politically Incorrect Humor to Top of the Box Office
item.description: The film took in $55 million over the weekend in the United States and Canada. It was the biggest opening for an R-rated comedy in 12 years.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes

item.title: 5 Takeaways From Scott Pelley’s Interview With The New York Times
item.description: Mr. Pelley, who was at CBS News for 37 years, including as a White House correspondent and a “60 Minutes” correspondent, spoke in his first extended interview since he was fired.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan
item.media:description: In his first sit-down interview since he was fired from CBS News, Scott Pelley talks about his recent experiences at the network.

item.title: OPEC Plus to Boost Oil Production as Ceasefire in Iran Remains Elusive
item.description: The cartel’s move to increase output by 188,000 barrels per day is largely symbolic, with vast amounts of the world’s oil stranded by the effective shutdown of the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.category: Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Prince Abdulaziz bin Salman al-Saud, Saudi Arabia’s minister of energy, left, and OPEC’s secretary general, Haitham Al Ghais at an economic forum in St. Petersburg, Russia, last week.

item.title: How People Working in Debt Collection Handle Abuse From Callers
item.description: More Americans are taking on debt and falling behind on bills. The people calling them to collect often endure a torrent of verbal abuse and threats of violence.
item.dc:creator: Kali Bomeli
item.media:credit: Whitten Sabbatini for The New York Times
item.media:description: Guybrielle Madison has worked as a debt collector for six years. She speaks to about 50 people each day, and being called racial slurs is common.

item.title: My Boss Is a Manipulative Creep. What Can I Do?
item.description: Plus: How much unpaid work can a recruiter demand? And what on earth is that smell?
item.dc:creator: Max Read
item.media:credit: Margeaux Walter for The New York Times

item.title: Daughter of Mrs. Fields Starts Fields Good, a Healthy Cookie Brand
item.description: Ashley Fields is a co-founder of Fields Good cookies. One variety contains protein, and others claim to boost brain performance or aid sleep.
item.dc:creator: Alisha Haridasani Gupta
item.media:credit: Jessica Attie for The New York Times
item.media:description: Growing up, “I didn’t learn how to bake with science-backed ingredients,” said Ashley Fields, a co-founder of Fields Good, an online cookie company.

item.title: As Oil Prices Spike, Talk of ‘Demand Destruction’ Sets In
item.description: The decades-old term refers to the sustained loss of demand for a commodity, caused by high prices.
item.dc:creator: Lora Kelley
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Christine Hunsicker, CaaStle CEO, Confessed to Fraud. The Board Let Her Stay in Charge.
item.description: The final months of the fashion tech start-up CaaStle included fake audits, stock buybacks and a damage control strategy led by the chief executive who defrauded it.
item.dc:creator: Anita Raghavan
item.media:description: Christine Hunsicker, CaaStle’s co-founder, pleaded guilty to securities fraud in March.

item.title: Alan Riding, Times Correspondent in Latin America and Paris, Dies at 82
item.description: He was a cosmopolitan observer and interpreter of societies he knew firsthand, whether writing about war in Nicaragua or the history and cultural salons of France.
item.dc:creator: Alan Cowell
item.category: Distant Neighbors: A Portrait of the Mexicans (Book)
item.media:description: Alan Riding around 1980, when he was the Mexico City bureau chief of The New York Times, interviewing rebel leaders in El Salvador.

item.title: ‘Talk to My A.I. Twin’: Busy Executives Have a New Productivity Hack
item.description: C.E.O.s and Harvard professors ae making A.I.-powered doubles that answer questions and attend meetings.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Kessler

item.title: Screwworm Flies Add to Cattle Ranchers’ Woes
item.description: Some Texans fear “the nightmares and the horrors” of a potential screwworm outbreak. Elsewhere, not enough grass to feed cattle sends them to market earlier.
item.dc:creator: Kevin Draper
item.media:description: The U.S. cattle herd is at its smallest size in 75 years, driving up the price of beef.

item.title: ‘60 Minutes’ Is a ‘Cage Full of Tigers.’ Can Nick Bilton Lead It?
item.description: The tech journalist and filmmaker was a surprise choice to lead a famously combative newsroom. His first week was chaos.
item.dc:creator: Katie Robertson
item.media:description: Nick Bilton, the executive producer of “60 Minutes.”

item.title: 5 Tips to Start Managing Your Aging Parents’ Money
item.description: And one big mistake to avoid.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Rabasca Roepe
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: Cathleen Tobin, a financial planner, has been assisting her father, Ed, with his money. “Helping your parents in small ways builds trust and normalizes your involvement,” she said.

item.title: Stocks Slide as Investors See Rates Rising After Strong Jobs Data
item.description: The S&P 500 fell more than 2.6 percent on Friday, its worst one-day drop of the year and ending nine weeks of gains.
item.dc:creator: Joe Rennison

item.title: ’60 Minutes’ Stars Will Stay After Pelley’s Firing Because They Don’t Want Show to ‘Die’
item.description: Lesley Stahl, Bill Whitaker and Jon Wertheim said on Friday in an email to the show’s staff that they had reached the decision after a period of “grieving” and frustration.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin and Michael M. Grynbaum

item.title: What to know about the report.
Central Ohio Becomes Hub for Tech and Manufacturing
item.description: Tech titans and Silicon Valley transplants changed the Columbus area, but not everyone is thrilled about the rapid transformation.
item.dc:creator: Farah Stockman
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times

item.title: Elon Musk Dominates List of Highest Paid C.E.O.s
item.description: Executive compensation is accelerating while rank-and-file workers lag, widening the pay gap in corporate America.
item.dc:creator: Jeff Sommer
item.category: Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act (2010)
item.media:credit: Photo illustration by Nicolas Ortega; photos by Getty Images

item.title: White House rejoices over strong jobs report.
item.description: The stronger-than-expected report offers President Trump a talking point for the midterms, even as it also reduces the odds that the Federal Reserve may cut rates.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm

item.title: More Renters Are Using Tools to Skip Security Deposits, but There’s a Catch
item.description: Third-party services charge fees to help renters bypass some upfront costs of signing a lease. Unlike traditional deposits, the money won’t be returned to tenants.
item.dc:creator: Ann Carrns

item.title: Anthropic’s Call for A.I. Nonproliferation
item.description: The artificial intelligence giant said a “brake pedal” was needed to protect humanity from self-improving models. The proposal could have big consequences.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Does the world need to consider a kind of A.I. nonproliferation treaty?

item.title: With Jobs Market Stable, Fed Is Focusing on Inflation Over Rate Cuts
item.description: Officials at the Federal Reserve have turned their focus to resurgent inflation.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith

item.title: U.S. Job Market Pushes Past Shocks and Strains
item.description: Employers added 172,000 jobs in May, adding to a vigorous pace of hiring in recent months. But wage growth is not keeping up with higher prices and consumers remain pessimistic.
item.dc:creator: Lydia DePillis

item.title: How the Job Market Is Leaving New Graduates Behind
item.description: Sydney Ember, a Times business reporter, has been speaking with recent college graduates struggling to find work. She explains why starting a career in the current economy could leave lasting scars on wages and opportunities.
item.dc:creator: Sydney Ember, Nour Idriss and Stephanie Swart

item.title: To Sell Trucks, Break Out the Cowboys and Wrap Them in Old Glory
item.description: Americans — especially American men — love trucks, and high gas prices aren’t swaying automakers. Ram has a new line of fuel-guzzling muscle trucks, and some in-your-face ads to sell them.
item.dc:creator: Roy Furchgott
item.media:description: The 2027 Ram 1500 Rumble Bee SRT. “Ram buyers like other people to know that they are driving a Ram truck,” one analyst said.

item.title: Crowded Airport Lounges Are Rolling Out Grab-and-Go Options
item.description: New premium pit stops are giving fliers a place for a quick bite and drink without having to line up for increasingly crowded traditional lounges.
item.dc:creator: Julie Weed

item.title: Can He-Man’s ‘Masters of the Universe’ Generate the Same Movie Magic as Barbie?
item.description: “Masters of the Universe” will test whether Mattel can cement itself as an entertainment powerhouse with a hypermasculine character from the 1980s.
item.dc:creator: Nicole Sperling

item.title: A Job Market Leaving Young Graduates Behind Could Scar Them for Years
item.description: The labor market has improved, but people entering the work force are having a harder time starting careers, a dynamic that has had permanent effects in the past.
item.dc:creator: Sydney Ember

item.title: When Jeffrey Epstein Needed Favors, This Restaurant Mogul Was There
item.description: Stephen Hanson, whose empire included Blue Water Grill and Ruby Foo’s, was a devoted friend and wingman who helped manage and entertain the women in Epstein’s orbit.
item.dc:creator: Kim Severson
